summary: A practical tutorial on embedding Entypo pictograph fonts directly into Qt applications as vector icons. The post walks through converting font files to C byte arrays using xxd, loading them via QFontDatabase, and rendering Unicode characters as styled button icons using C++11 literals.
key_points: Vector pictograph fonts like Entypo can replace bitmap icons in Qt apps for cleaner, scalable graphics
The xxd tool converts font files into C include files containing hex byte arrays
QFontDatabase::addApplicationFontFromData allows embedding fonts directly in the binary without external resource files
QApplication must be initialized before calling addApplicationFontFromData to avoid segfaults
Qt's rcc resource system is actually the superior approach for embedding binary data (acknowledged in the edit)
money_quotes: Once you learn about Entypo, you see these pictographs everywhere!
One very important note here, make sure you create your QApplication object before you call addApplicationFontFromData otherwise you will get a segfault. I figured this out the hard way.
It was mentioned to me that it is better to use rcc instead of xxd for storing binaries in a Qt app. This is superior, so don't use my silly silly way.
key_insight: Embedding vector font files directly as byte arrays in C++ code provides a simple way to add scalable icons to Qt applications without external resource dependencies, though Qt's built-in resource compiler (rcc) is the more idiomatic solution.
